summary: Microsoft documents SpyNetReporting on the Defender Spynet policy path. In a clean Win25H2Clean trace, SecurityHealthService.exe first saw the policy path as absent, then read SpyNetReporting = 2 directly from the policy path after the write.

setting.name: Microsoft Defender MAPS Membership
setting.casual_explanation: Microsoft Defender can join Microsoft MAPS so the service can use cloud lookup and share more threat data with Microsoft.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change this when they want stronger cloud-backed Defender checks or when they are comparing Defender cloud-policy behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Joining MAPS can improve cloud-backed protection, but it also sends more Defender telemetry and sample metadata to Microsoft.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents SpyNetReporting on HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet. The ADMX-backed policy surface lists 0 = disabled, 1 = basic membership, and 2 = advanced membership. Microsoft also notes that on modern Windows builds value 1 or 2 can both land in advanced membership behavior. In the clean 25H2 VM snapshot, SecurityHealthService.exe first opened the policy branch and got NAME NOT FOUND, then fell back to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet\SpyNetReporting = 2. After writing SpyNetReporting = 2 on the policy branch, the same service read Data: 2 directly from the policy path.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Leave the MAPS membership policy unset and let Defender use its current managed baseline.

allowed_values.label: MAPS disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Turns off the documented Defender MAPS policy surface.

allowed_values.label: Documented membership value 1
allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents this as the lower MAPS membership value, but newer builds can collapse 1 and 2 into the same advanced behavior.

allowed_values.label: Advanced membership
allowed_values.meaning: Uses the documented advanced membership value that SecurityHealthService.exe read directly in the 25H2 VM.

targets.notes: The runtime proof is strongest for value 2. The app stays on the documented advanced-membership value and does not need to model the weaker 1-versus-2 split.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes SpyNetReporting = 2 on the documented Defender Spynet policy path.

windows_defaults.label: Policy unset baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where the Defender MAPS policy is not configured on the local policy path

states.rationale: Leave the Defender Spynet policy branch unset.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where Defender cloud-membership policy should stay unmanaged
avoid_for: People explicitly comparing MAPS policy behavior

states.rationale: Keep the policy unset until there is a reason to force a specific MAPS membership value.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the least-assumptive Defender baseline on this policy surface.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to force advanced MAPS membership for a controlled comparison.
tradeoffs: Does not force a specific MAPS membership mode.

recommended_profiles.label: Advanced MAPS membership
avoid_for: People who do not want extra Defender cloud telemetry

states.rationale: Use the documented advanced membership value that the Win25H2Clean trace read back directly.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the strongest documented MAPS membership setting for a controlled Defender comparison.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the default unmanaged Defender state or you do not want extra cloud telemetry.
tradeoffs: Forces the documented advanced MAPS membership value.
Leaves the lower 1-versus-2 membership split out of scope for the app.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: ADMX_MicrosoftDefenderAntivirus SpynetReporting
evidence.summary: Microsoft maps SpyNetReporting to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet and documents 0 = disabled, 1 = basic membership, and 2 = advanced membership. The page also notes that on modern Windows builds value 1 or 2 can enroll the device into advanced membership behavior.

evidence.title: Local Defender MAPS lead note
evidence.summary: The local lead note tracks SpyNetReporting on HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet with 0, 1, and 2 as the documented values.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon baseline for Defender MAPS policy path
evidence.summary: On the clean high-risk snapshot, SecurityHealthService.exe opened HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet and got NAME NOT FOUND, then fell back to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet\SpyNetReporting and read Data: 2.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon read for SpyNetReporting = 2
evidence.summary: After writing SpyNetReporting = 2 on the Defender Spynet policy path, SecurityHealthService.exe queried the same value and read Data: 2 directly from the policy branch.

evidence.title: Current security provider MAPS membership write

decision.why: Microsoft documents the exact Spynet policy path and the advanced membership value 2, and the Win25H2Clean Procmon trace shows SecurityHealthService.exe reading SpyNetReporting = 2 from that path. The app only exposes the documented value 2, so the unresolved 1-versus-2 split is not a blocker for this record.

open_questions: Do current 25H2 Defender builds still distinguish value 1 from value 2 in a visible way, or do both land in the same effective advanced membership state?
Should the app ever expose a lower MAPS membership value, or should it stay on the documented advanced membership value only?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: SecurityHealthService.exe | RegQueryValue | HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet\SpyNetReporting | SUCCESS | Type: REG_DWORD, Length: 4, Data: 2
validation_proof.notes: The clean baseline first showed the Defender Spynet policy branch as absent and the non-policy live store reading SpyNetReporting = 2. After the policy write, SecurityHealthService.exe read the same value directly from the policy path.
